metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Foundations of Mathematics and Pre-calculus 10 is the primary academic mathematics course for students pursuing post-secondary academic or technical pathways. Students extend algebraic thinking to include powers, polynomial operations and factoring, linear functions, trigonometry, and systems of equations. This course opens pathways to both Pre-calculus 11 and Foundations of Mathematics 11.
metadata.general_description: The primary academic Grade 10 mathematics course covering algebra, powers, polynomial operations, linear functions, trigonometry, systems of equations, and financial literacy — opening pathways to Pre-calculus 11 and Foundations of Mathematics 11.

big_ideas.statement: Algebra allows us to generalize relationships through abstract thinking.
big_ideas.elaboration: Algebraic notation and reasoning allow us to describe and analyze relationships between quantities generally — not just for specific numbers — enabling powerful generalizations and problem-solving.

big_ideas.statement: The meanings of, and connections between, each operation extend to powers and polynomials.
big_ideas.elaboration: The arithmetic operations students understand with numbers extend naturally to powers with integral exponents and polynomial expressions — following the same structural rules and enabling new algebraic capabilities.

big_ideas.statement: Constant rate of change is an essential attribute of linear relations and has meaning in different representations and contexts.
big_ideas.elaboration: The slope of a linear function represents a constant rate of change — a foundational concept that connects tables, graphs, and equations and appears across mathematics, science, and everyday situations.

big_ideas.statement: Trigonometry involves using proportional reasoning to solve indirect measurement problems.
big_ideas.elaboration: The trigonometric ratios in right triangles are ratios between side lengths — a proportional relationship that enables the indirect measurement of distances and angles that cannot be measured directly.

big_ideas.statement: Representing and analyzing situations allows us to notice and wonder about relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Multiple representations — tables, graphs, equations, and verbal descriptions — provide different windows into mathematical relationships, enabling deeper understanding and problem-solving.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and other tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, strategic thinking about number; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively and with curiosity when exploring problems.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply thinking strategies to solve algebraic and geometric problems.
I can use technology (graphing calculators, Desmos) to explore algebraic and trigonometric relationships.
I can estimate and reason about the reasonableness of algebraic and numerical results.
I can model real-world situations using linear functions, systems of equations, and polynomial expressions.

competencies.raw: Develop, demonstrate, and apply mathematical understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore and illustrate mathematical concepts; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve problems with persistence and positive disposition; engage in problem-solving connected with place, story, cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can develop and demonstrate understanding of algebra and functions through inquiry and problem solving.
I can visualize and sketch linear functions, right triangles, and algebraic relationships.
I can apply flexible strategies including substitution, elimination, and graphing to solve systems of equations.
I can solve problems with persistence, connecting mathematics to real-world and cultural contexts.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in classroom discussions; take risks when offering ideas in classroom discourse.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify algebraic reasoning and solutions in multiple ways.
I can represent functions using tables, graphs, equations, and verbal descriptions.
I can use accurate mathematical vocabulary when discussing algebraic and geometric concepts.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my algebraic and mathematical thinking and identify areas for growth.
I can connect the mathematics in this course to science, technology, and my own interests.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical knowledge and perspectives into my thinking.

areas.name: Powers and Number
areas.raw: Operations on powers with integral exponents; prime factorization.
topics: Exponent laws: product law, quotient law, power of a power, zero exponent, negative exponents
Integral exponents: positive, negative, and zero
Powers in scientific notation: multiplication and division
Prime factorization: GCF and LCM applications

areas.name: Polynomials
areas.raw: Multiplication of polynomial expressions; polynomial factoring.
topics: Polynomial terminology: degree, leading coefficient, constant term
Multiplication of polynomials: distributive property, FOIL, and special products
Factoring: common factor, trinomials (simple and complex ax²+bx+c), difference of squares
Factoring as the inverse of expansion: verifying by multiplying

areas.name: Functions and Linear Relations
areas.raw: Functions and relations; linear functions and slope; arithmetic sequences.
topics: Functions and relations: definition, function notation f(x), domain and range
Slope: positive, negative, zero, and undefined; calculating slope from two points
Equation forms of a line: slope-intercept y = mx + b, point-slope, general form Ax + By + C = 0
Graphing linear functions and identifying intercepts
Arithmetic sequences: common difference, general term formula tₙ = t₁ + (n-1)d

areas.name: Systems of Linear Equations
topics: Solving systems by graphing: intersection of two lines
Solving by substitution
Solving by elimination (addition/subtraction method)
Contextual problems: setting up and solving systems from word problems
Identifying number of solutions: one, none, infinitely many

areas.name: Trigonometry
topics: Right triangle trigonometry: SOH-CAH-TOA
Finding missing sides using sine, cosine, and tangent
Finding missing angles using inverse trigonometric ratios
Solving right triangle problems: angles of elevation and depression
Applications: surveying, navigation, and construction contexts

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Financial literacy: gross and net pay.
topics: Employment income: hourly wages, salary, commission, piecework
Payroll deductions: income tax, CPP, EI
Gross vs. net pay: computing take-home pay
